title: The Nervous System and Music: How Sound Regulates the Autonomic System
excerpt: Explore how music influences the autonomic nervous system to support trauma healing and nervous system regulation.
body:
Introduction: The Science of Sound and the Body

We’ve all experienced how music can shift our mood, slow a racing heart, or energize a sluggish afternoon. But these aren’t just subjective feelings — sound has a profound effect on our nervous system, specifically the autonomic nervous system (ANS), which governs our survival responses and internal regulation. For those doing self-directed healing work or recovering from trauma, understanding how music interacts with the ANS reveals a powerful tool for nervous system regulation and somatic healing.

Leading trauma researchers like Bessel van der Kolk and Stephen Porges have shown us that trauma isn’t just stored in the mind but deeply imprinted in the body’s nervous system. Music taps into this body-based experience, activating neural pathways that regulate stress, safety, and connection.

Understanding the Autonomic Nervous System

The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary bodily functions like heart rate, digestion, respiration, and arousal states. It has two main branches:

  • Sympathetic nervous system (SNS): Responsible for the "fight, flight, or freeze" response — increasing heart rate, tensing muscles, and mobilizing energy.
  • Parasympathetic nervous system (PNS): Supports "rest, digest, and repair" functions — slowing heart rate, relaxing muscles, and promoting calm.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory adds nuance by identifying the vagus nerve’s two pathways: a ventral vagal complex associated with social engagement and safety, and a dorsal vagal complex linked to shutdown and immobilization (Porges, 2011). This perspective is key to understanding how music can promote feelings of safety or trigger defensive reactions.

How Music Engages the Nervous System

Music is essentially patterned sound waves that the body interprets not just as noise but as signals with emotional and physiological meaning. Researchers have demonstrated that music affects the nervous system in several ways:

  • Modulating heart rate and breathing: Rhythmic elements can entrain breathing and heart rhythms, promoting autonomic balance (Bernardi et al., 2006).
  • Activating the vagus nerve: Certain frequencies and tempos stimulate the vagal pathways that support parasympathetic activation and social engagement (Porges, 2017).
  • Releasing neurochemicals: Music triggers dopamine, oxytocin, and endorphins, which regulate mood and bonding (Salimpoor et al., 2011).
  • Engaging brain circuits involved in emotion: The limbic system and prefrontal cortex respond to music, influencing emotional regulation and memory (Koelsch, 2014).

Music, Polyvagal Theory, and Trauma Recovery

Trauma often disrupts the autonomic nervous system’s ability to regulate, locking individuals into hyperarousal (sympathetic dominance) or hypoarousal (dorsal vagal shutdown). Music can help recalibrate these states by providing a safe auditory environment that cues the ventral vagal complex.

Deb Dana, a clinical social worker and pioneer in applying Polyvagal Theory to therapy, notes that music with a calm tempo and predictable rhythm can help clients shift from defensive states to a regulated state of safety and social engagement (Dana, 2018). This is crucial for trauma survivors who often feel trapped in fight, flight, or freeze.

“Music provides a bridge between the external world and our internal nervous system, offering a path back to safety when trauma has disrupted that connection.” – Deb Dana

Scientific Evidence: Studies on Music and Autonomic Regulation

Multiple studies have documented the physiological effects of music on the ANS:

  • Heart rate variability (HRV): HRV is a marker of autonomic flexibility and vagal tone. Research shows that listening to slow-tempo music increases HRV, indicating improved parasympathetic activation (Thaut et al., 2015).
  • Stress hormone reduction: Music listening lowers cortisol levels, reducing the body’s stress response (Chanda & Levitin, 2013).
  • Improved mood and anxiety: Randomized controlled trials have found that music interventions reduce anxiety and depressive symptoms by modulating autonomic function (Maratos et al., 2008).
  • Neurological trauma: Studies on post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) patients found that music therapy improves autonomic regulation and reduces symptoms by engaging the vagal system (Carr et al., 2012).

Practical Ways to Use Music for Nervous System Regulation

Knowing how music influences the nervous system allows you to use sound intentionally in your healing practice. Here are practical strategies supported by research and clinical experience:

  • Choose calming, steady-tempo music: Aim for music around 60-80 beats per minute to promote parasympathetic activation (Bernardi et al., 2006). Avoid sudden loud sounds or irregular rhythms that might trigger alarm.
  • Use music for breath entrainment: Match your breathing to the rhythm of the music to enhance vagal tone and relax the nervous system.
  • Incorporate music into somatic practices: Combine music with movement, yoga, or body scans to deepen regulation and embodiment.
  • Personalize your playlist: Music that feels safe and evokes positive memories can strengthen social engagement pathways (Porges, 2017).
  • Experiment with active vs. passive listening: Try singing or humming along to engage the vagus nerve through vocalization, a technique highlighted by Gabor Maté for nervous system regulation (Maté, 2018).

Limitations and Considerations

While music can be a powerful regulatory tool, it’s not a cure-all. For those with complex trauma, certain sounds or music may trigger dysregulation or flashbacks. Pat Ogden’s sensorimotor psychotherapy emphasizes careful titration and attunement to body responses when introducing somatic tools like music (Ogden et al., 2006).

Additionally, individual differences in musical preferences and cultural contexts shape how music affects the nervous system. It’s important to listen to your body’s reactions and adjust your choices accordingly.

Conclusion: Music as a Somatic Bridge to Regulation

Trauma and dysregulation live in the nervous system, but so does the capacity for healing and resilience. Music offers a unique, accessible pathway to engage the autonomic nervous system, supporting nervous system regulation and somatic healing. Rooted in science and clinical wisdom, sound is more than an art form — it’s a nervous system tool.

By understanding and utilizing music’s impact on the sympathetic and parasympathetic branches, you can empower yourself to shift states, reduce stress, and cultivate safety in your body. Whether you’re navigating trauma recovery or simply seeking deeper nervous system balance, music can be a trusted companion on your healing journey.

References

  • Bernardi, L., Porta, C., & Sleight, P. (2006). Cardiovascular, cerebrovascular, and respiratory changes induced by different types of music in musicians and non-musicians: The importance of silence. Heart, 92(4), 445–452.
  • Carr, C., Odell-Miller, H., Priebe, S., & McDermott, O. (2012). Music therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, (12).
  • Chanda, M. L., & Levitin, D. J. (2013). The neurochemistry of music. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 17(4), 179–193.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Koelsch, S. (2014). Brain correlates of music-evoked emotions. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 15(3), 170–180.
  • Maratos, A. S., Gold, C., Wang, X., & Crawford, M. J. (2008). Music therapy for depression. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, (1).
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: Exploring the Stress-Disease Connection. Wiley.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The polyvagal theory: Neurophysiological foundations of emotions, attachment, communication, and self-regulation. Norton Series on Interpersonal Neurobiology.
  • Porges, S. W. (2017). The pocket guide to the polyvagal theory: The transformative power of feeling safe. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Salimpoor, V. N., Benovoy, M., Larcher, K., Dagher, A., & Zatorre, R. J. (2011). Anatomically distinct dopamine release during anticipation and experience of peak emotion to music. Nature Neuroscience, 14(2), 257–262.
  • Thaut, M. H., Gardiner, J. C., Holmberg, D., Horwitz, J., Kent, L., Andrews, G., & Hoemberg, V. (2015). Neurologic music therapy in stroke rehabilitation. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1252(1), 262–266.

faqs.q: How does music influence the autonomic nervous system?
faqs.a: Music affects the autonomic nervous system by modulating heart rate, breathing, and activating the vagus nerve, which supports relaxation and social engagement.

faqs.q: Can music help with trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Yes, music can help trauma survivors by promoting parasympathetic activation and creating a sense of safety, which is essential for nervous system regulation.

faqs.q: What types of music are best for nervous system regulation?
faqs.a: Slow-tempo, steady rhythm music around 60-80 beats per minute is generally best to promote relaxation and parasympathetic activity.

faqs.q: Are there any risks in using music for nervous system regulation?
faqs.a: For some, especially those with complex trauma, certain music may trigger dysregulation. It's important to observe your body's responses and choose music that feels safe.

affiliateProducts.description: Deb Dana's book is essential for understanding how to apply Polyvagal Theory in healing, with practical guidance on using rhythm and sound for nervous system regulation.
